---
title: "Research"
description: "Single-cell transcriptomics and AI methods for interpretable, generalizable biology."
---

I develop computational and machine-learning methods for **single-cell RNA-seq**, with a
focus on making the analysis **interpretable**, **reproducible**, and **generalizable**
across datasets. The thread running through my work: high-dimensional single-cell data is
most useful when we can reduce it to a small, trustworthy set of signals — the responsive
cell populations, the genes that matter, the predictable response to a perturbation.

## Research interests

### Biomarker & gene-panel discovery
Finding compact, generalizable gene panels that explain a biological or disease state from
single-cell data. This is the focus of my published tool **[scPanel](projects/index.qmd)**, which
identifies the cell population(s) most responsive to a perturbation and then selects a
sparse panel (often fewer than 20 genes) for robust, cross-dataset patient classification.

### AI/ML methods for perturbation & disease-state modeling
Building and applying machine-learning models that predict how cells respond to
perturbations (disease, drugs, genetic changes), and that transfer reliably to data they
were not trained on. I'm especially interested in methods whose outputs are biologically
interpretable rather than black boxes.

### Reproducible & generalizable single-cell analysis
Designing pipelines and tooling — built on the scanpy / anndata ecosystem — that make
single-cell analyses transparent, repeatable, and easy for collaborators to reuse.
Generalization across datasets, batches, and conditions is a first-class goal, not an
afterthought.

## Direction

My longer-term aim is to bridge **method development** and **biological/clinical
application**: methods that are rigorous and reusable, validated on real biological
questions, and packaged so that biologists can apply them to their own data. I'm actively
looking to [collaborate](index.qmd) with both wet-lab groups and method developers.

<!-- TODO: refine direction once newer projects are published; keep unpublished work off this page. -->
